text: The siege engines groaned forward at his signal.
He kissed the pommel before returning the blade to its scabbard.
Dawn broke, and the banners were already marching south.
A prince does not weep in front of his father's men.
The crown sat heavy upon his brow during the oath-taking.
He rode at the head of the column, visor raised.
The envoy knelt, and Edmund bade him rise.
Commands went out in Latin, then again in the common tongue.
The hunting party returned at dusk with a stag slung across the packhorse.
He signed the writ without reading it and pressed his seal into the wax.
The war council argued until the candles burned low.
Henry accepted the sword his father had carried at Crécy.
Trumpets sounded as the young lord entered the hall.
He drained his cup and called for more wine.
The herald named him heir before the assembled barons.
Armored retainers followed him through the gatehouse.
He inspected the cavalry at first light, saying little.
The treaty was read aloud, and he nodded once.
His falcon struck the heron mid-flight over the water meadow.
Ransom would be paid, or the hostages would hang by Michaelmas.
At the tournament's close, he presented the prize of arms himself.
The chancellor whispered something, and he laughed without warmth.
He dismounted at the cathedral steps and walked bareheaded to the altar.
The standard of his house snapped against the northern wind.
Judgment was passed from the high seat before dusk.

The campaign had dragged on into its third winter. His captains were restless, his coffers thinning, and the Burgundians still held the river crossing. He summoned the council at Vespers and listened without speaking as each man argued. When the last had finished, he rose, set down his wine, and named the three who would cross with him at dawn. No one asked what would happen to the rest. By the time the chapel bells rang Matins, the camp was already moving under torchlight.

He had been raised for this moment since the age of seven — the sword lessons, the Latin lessons, the long afternoons watching his father hold court from the high seat. Now the crown was his, and the great hall seemed smaller than he remembered. The earls came one by one to kneel and speak their vows. He gave each the same measured nod, the same steady gaze. Afterward, alone in the solar, he poured himself a cup and stared into the fire until the logs collapsed into embers.

The stag had been running for over an hour when the hounds finally brought it to bay in a clearing above the beech wood. He dismounted, accepted the boar-spear from his master of hunt, and approached on foot as custom demanded. The kill was clean, as his father had always taught him. Afterward, the horn sounded the mort, and the pages began the work of gralloching. He wiped his hands on a cloth and drank the wine that was offered without ceremony.

News came from the south before breakfast: the Duke of Anjou had crossed the border with three thousand horse. He read the dispatch twice, then folded it and slid it across the table to his marshal. They spoke in low tones over bread and cheese. By noon the summons had gone out to every sworn man between the river and the sea. By nightfall the castle yard was full of armed retainers, and the forges had already begun to ring.

He preferred the company of his falconers to that of his courtiers, and spent most mornings on the downs above the castle when the weather allowed. The birds knew his voice, and the oldest of them, a gyrfalcon named Blanche, would settle on his wrist without the glove's encouragement. Today she had taken two pigeons and a young duck. He walked back to the castle on foot, leading his horse, letting the morning stretch as long as it could before duty called him inside.

The coronation feast ran into its fourth hour before he allowed himself to step away from the high table. His new queen smiled at him as he rose, and the kitchen boys began bringing out the subtlety: a sugar-spun castle taller than a man. He walked the length of the hall slowly, acknowledging each noble house in turn. None of them needed to know that his boots were killing him, or that he had barely slept in three days. The smile stayed fixed exactly where it needed to be.

They had ridden hard for a day and a night to reach the abbey before the old man died. He knelt beside the bed and took the thin hand in both of his own. His father could no longer speak, but the eyes were clear, and they held his. The abbot stood at the foot of the bed, reading the prayers for the dying. Outside, the retinue waited in the rain. When it was over, he walked out to them and said only what needed to be said.

The embassy from Aragon had arrived with forty horses, three chests of gifts, and a proposal of marriage for his sister. He received them in the lesser hall, not the great one — a deliberate signal that the matter was not yet settled. Wine was poured. Courtesies were exchanged in slow, measured Latin. He listened more than he spoke, and when the ambassador finally stated the dowry figure, he allowed the silence to stretch uncomfortably long before he answered. That, too, was deliberate.

At the tournament he rode against the Earl of Westmorland in the third pass, and both lances shattered. The crowd roared. He circled his horse at the end of the list, handed the broken stump to his squire, and accepted a fresh lance without looking at it. His father watched from the high seat, expressionless. His mother, he noticed, was no longer watching at all. On the fourth pass he unhorsed the earl cleanly, and the thunder of applause was the only answer he needed.

The winter council dragged into its second week without resolution. Supplies were short, the roads were closed, and his cousin's claim to the northern march grew louder with each passing day. He heard the arguments patiently, as a sovereign must. But on the evening of the ninth day he stopped the discussion mid-sentence and announced the decision he had made three days before. The lords looked at one another. No one argued. By morning, riders were already heading north with his seal on their letters.

He rose before the household, as was his habit, and walked the battlements alone with a cup of warmed wine. From the eastern tower he could see the river, silver in the early light, and the smoke already rising from the village beyond the mill. A hawk turned slowly on the wind above the keep. For a quarter of an hour there was nothing to decide, nothing to sign, nothing to weigh. Then the chapel bell rang, and the day began in earnest.

They brought the prisoner in at midnight, bound and bareheaded. He had refused to have the man questioned until he could see him himself. The interrogation was short. The rebel lord answered his questions directly, without flinching, and offered no plea. When it was done, he rose, nodded to the guards, and left the chamber without speaking further. The sentence would be announced in the morning, after Mass, in the presence of the full court. There was no point in doing it any other way.

The army had been camped outside the walls of the city for six weeks when the gates finally opened. He accepted the mayor's surrender at the edge of the moat, on horseback, with his captains arrayed behind him. The terms had been negotiated for three days. The city would keep its privileges. The garrison would be allowed to march out under arms. The cathedral would not be sacked. He had insisted on each of these points personally, against the advice of his marshal, and he would live with the consequences of that insistence for the rest of his reign.

His tutor had been a Dominican from Paris, sharp-tongued and fond of long silences. The lessons had covered Aristotle, Cicero, the Gospels, and a good deal of military history. He had not enjoyed them at the time. Now, ten years later, riding to parley with a rival he did not trust, he found himself remembering a particular passage from Livy about the folly of underestimating a wounded enemy. He slowed his horse and turned to his captain. They would approach the meeting place from the north, he said, not the west.

The young lord's wedding was to take place at Midsummer, and the castle had been full of builders and tapestry-makers for months. He walked through the great hall in the late afternoon, inspecting the new hangings, and stopped at the window that looked down onto the inner court. His bride-to-be was there, laughing with her women, quite unaware that she was being watched. He stood there for a long moment. Then he went back to his chamber and returned to the work that was waiting for him on the desk.

At Easter he made his procession to the cathedral on foot, as his grandfather had done, as his great-grandfather had done, and as every king of his line had done since the charter. The road was lined with the common folk of the city, pressed against the timbers. He did not look at them directly, but he knew they were watching, and that was the point of the ritual. Afterward, at the high altar, he received the sacrament from the archbishop's own hand. The bells rang until his ears ached.

The cavalry charge broke against the pike hedge like a wave against a cliff. He watched from the low hill to the east with his bannermen around him, and he understood within a heartbeat that the day was going badly. He gave the order for the reserve, then for the archers, then for himself. When he rode down into the field with his household knights, the standard-bearer already half-dead from a crossbow bolt, he knew the chroniclers would describe this moment however they pleased. What mattered was what happened next.

He spent the morning in the kennels, as he often did when the weight of the court became too much. The huntsman had bred a new litter from the bitch who had served him so well on the boar hunt two autumns ago. He knelt in the straw and watched the pups climb over one another. For perhaps an hour, no one came to find him. When the page finally arrived with news from the chancellor, he rose, brushed the straw from his hose, and walked back to the palace without hurry.

The papal legate had arrived from Avignon with an entourage of forty and an appetite for fine wine. He received the man with every courtesy that protocol demanded, and not a thimbleful more. They dined together twice, hunted together once, and spoke of the king's obligations to the Holy See at great length. When the legate finally left, he stood on the steps and watched the procession disappear down the road. Then he turned to his chancellor and said, very quietly, that certain letters would need to be rewritten before morning.

His son was born in the third hour after Matins, a healthy boy with a strong cry. He had been pacing the antechamber for most of the night, despite his attendants' insistence that this was not his place. When the midwife finally opened the door, he went in without speaking and stood beside the bed. His wife looked up at him with an exhausted half-smile. He took the child from the nurse and held him carefully, the way his own father had once held him. He did not trust himself to speak for some time.

The plague had reached the city by late summer, and the court had withdrawn to the hunting lodge in the hills. He rode down once a week to meet with the chancellor at the halfway manor, and the rest of his days were spent in the library, the chapel, or the forest. The household was smaller than he had ever known it. For the first time in his life he had time to think without interruption. He was not sure, yet, whether this was a blessing or a punishment.

He was a younger son, and he had never expected to wear the crown. The smallpox took his brother in the autumn of his twenty-third year, and the council summoned him home from the garrison in Calais within a week. He arrived road-dirty and unprepared, and he took the oath three days later with his father's sword still too heavy in his hand. A decade on, the sword fit better. The weight of everything behind it had never grown any lighter.

The tournament at Smithfield was the grandest the kingdom had seen in thirty years, and he had insisted on riding despite the objections of his queen and his doctors. He broke three lances on the first day, four on the second, and unhorsed the champion of France on the third. That night, in his pavilion, he let the servants remove his armor in silence. His shoulder was badly bruised. His ribs were worse. He said nothing to anyone, drank a cup of wine, and slept for fourteen hours straight.

The rebellion in the north had been put down, and the ringleaders had been brought in chains to the castle. He sat through the judgments for two days without eating, listening to each case as it was presented, each plea for mercy, each accusation of treachery. He was not a cruel man. He was also not a weak one. On the morning of the third day he announced the sentences from the high seat. Seven were hanged. Four were exiled. The rest were returned to their lands with heavy fines and new overlords.

In the quiet hour after Compline he would often go to the private chapel alone, leaving the chaplain to his own devotions elsewhere. He did not always pray, though sometimes he did. Mostly he sat on the low bench near the rood screen and allowed his mind to empty. The stone was cold. The candles guttered in the draught from the window. It was the only time in the day when no one was asking him for anything, and he had come to treasure it the way other men treasured their mistresses or their wine.
The nets were mended by the time the tide turned.
He split the last of the winter wood before noon.
Thomas spat into his palms and took up the scythe again.
The ox leaned into the yoke, and the furrow deepened.
He left for the market before the cock had crowed twice.
The blacksmith wiped his forehead with the back of a sooty wrist.
Rain soaked his jerkin before he reached the upper field.
The catch had been poor, and he said so to his brother.
He stacked the hay until his arms wouldn't lift another forkful.
The miller took his tenth of the grain and said nothing.
He mended the fence where the ewe had broken through.
Will paid the ferryman a farthing and stepped aboard.
The apprentice burned his hand on the bellows again.
He hauled the creel up onto the pebbles and counted the eels.
The pig was slaughtered before Michaelmas, as was the custom.
He traded two hens for a length of good linen.
The roof wanted thatching before the next hard frost.
He walked behind the plough from sunrise until the angelus.
The cooper tapped the last hoop into place with satisfaction.
His boots were wet through by the time he reached the alehouse.
He cursed the weevils and turned the grain again.
The wheel needed greasing, and he knelt in the mud to see to it.
Jack shouldered the bundle of firewood and turned for home.
He tallied the tithe loaves on the lintel with a bit of charcoal.
The boat took on water before they reached the harbour mouth.

The harvest had been middling, and the tithe left little enough behind. He and his brother worked the last of the barley into shocks before the rain came in earnest. By sundown they were soaked through, their boots heavy with mud. Mother had the pottage ready when they ducked through the door, and the three of them ate in silence. There would be enough to see them to Candlemas, he reckoned. After that, God alone knew.

The forge had been his father's before him, and his grandfather's before that. He worked the bellows with the same rhythm he had learned as a boy, and the iron answered the hammer the same as it ever had. Today's order was a pair of plough-shares for the manor. Good work, steady coin. The apprentice fetched charcoal from the yard while he worked the glow into the shape he needed. The sparks fell on the stone floor and went out one by one.

They had put out before first light, as they always did when the wind was from the south. By mid-morning the nets were full, and by noon they were turning for home with a good haul of mackerel and a few bass for the inn. The sea was kind today. It had not always been. His elder brother had gone down off the headland the winter before last, and the memory came to him unbidden as he coiled the lines. He said a short prayer under his breath and did not mention it to the others.

Market day at the county town meant a walk of six miles each way, and he set out with his son before the sky had begun to pale. They had three geese and a sack of woollens to sell, and a list of things to bring back: salt, an iron pot if the price was right, and a ribbon for his wife's saint's day. The boy had never been to town before and could not stop staring at everything. By the time they walked home that evening, the lad had fallen silent, footsore and overwhelmed. He carried him the last mile.

The master carpenter had set him to work on the new roof beams, and he had been at the adze since dawn. The scent of fresh oak was sharp in the morning air. His shoulders ached in the familiar way, the way that meant good honest labor and not injury. At the bells for midday he stopped, drank water from the earthenware jug, and ate the bread and cheese his wife had packed. Then he picked up the adze again. There were another six beams to rough out before dusk.

The sheep had to be brought down from the high pasture before the weather turned, and he and the dog started up the slope just after dawn. The ewes were in no hurry to move. They never were. He whistled the old signals, the dog worked the edges of the flock, and slowly the whole group began to drift down the hill. By midafternoon they were all safely in the fold. He closed the gate, sat down on a stone, and chewed slowly on a heel of rye bread. The dog watched him with her tongue lolling.

The alehouse was crowded after the harvest was in. He found a space on the bench near the fire and accepted a mug from the host without asking the price — he knew it well enough. The talk was of the weather, the price of grain, and the reeve's new horse, which was said to have cost more than the reeve was worth. He laughed at that. A man does not laugh as often as he should, at his age, and he meant to enjoy it when he could. Tomorrow there would be fences to mend.

The boat had been his father's, and it was showing its age. He had patched the strakes again over the winter, and he had replaced the thwart with a length of good ash he had traded for at the spring fair. Even so, she leaked, and he was not sure how many more seasons she had in her. The thought of commissioning a new one from the boatwright was not one he could easily entertain. But he could not fish without a boat, either. He sat on the beach in the twilight and looked at her without speaking.

The tax collector had come through the village in the week before Lammas, and he had had to sell a yearling heifer to meet the demand. It was not the first time. It would not be the last. He walked the boy and the calf to the market at dawn, and walked back alone by mid-afternoon with a small purse of coin and a grim expression. His wife looked at him when he came through the door, and she did not need to ask. She set a bowl of broth in front of him instead.

The parish church needed its bell-rope replaced, and the priest had asked him to do the work as his contribution for the season. He spent two evenings braiding the new rope from good hemp in the barn, and on the third morning he climbed the tower with a lad from the next farm to help him. The old rope came down in a shower of dust and frayed strands. The new one went up stiff and fragrant. When they rang the midday bell from the chamber below, it sounded exactly as it should. He allowed himself a small satisfaction.

The mill-wheel had stopped turning in the heavy frost of January, and the villagers had been grumbling for a fortnight about the flour. He had ridden the six miles to the next parish with two sacks of grain on the packhorse, and he had come back after nightfall, cold to the bone, with the flour and a few items his wife had asked him to pick up. The frost had not broken. But the bread would be good again tomorrow, and that was something.

The apprentice had been with him for nearly four years now, and the boy was finally beginning to shape a decent horseshoe. He had watched him work all morning without saying much. Correction was best given sparingly, and only when necessary. When the lad finished his third shoe of the day, he picked it up, turned it slowly in his hand, and nodded once. It was the highest praise he was likely to give. The apprentice returned to the anvil with a visible lightness in his step.

The pig-killing was done every year in the week before Martinmas, and the whole family gathered for it. He was the one with the knife, as his father had been before him. The work was bloody and quick, and afterwards there was the long day of scalding, scraping, and butchering, and then the smoking and salting of what would see them through the lean months. By evening he smelled of pork fat and woodsmoke, and he slept the sleep of a man who has done a necessary thing well.

He had never been further than the county town until the year he was hired to go with the carter's train to the port. Three days each way on the road, sleeping in the straw of coaching inns, and a full day at the docks unloading wool and loading back with wine and iron. The sea had astonished him — he had not known it would be so loud. He came home a quieter man than he had gone, and his wife remarked on it. He told her about the sea, but not very well.

The storm had blown the roof off the byre, and he had spent the whole of the first fair day patching it. It was rough work, alone with a ladder and a pile of reed. By late afternoon his shoulders were ruined and his hands were raw. But the byre was weather-tight again, and the cow would be dry that night. He sat on the bench outside the cottage afterwards and let his wife bring him small beer while he watched the sun go down over the pasture. A man could do worse, he thought.

The abbot's man came through at Lammas to collect the rent, as he did every year, and the transaction was as cold and formal as it always was. He paid in coin where he could and in kind where he could not, and the clerk wrote it all down in the little book he carried. Afterwards, the clerk and the abbot's man rode on to the next holding. He watched them go from the gate, and he did not speak for a while. There were other holdings, on other land, that paid less. He knew it. But this was the land he had.

The flood had come up faster than anyone expected, and half the lower fields were under water by Prime. He and three of the neighbors drove the cattle to the higher ground above the road, shouting and slapping flanks, the dogs working the stragglers. By the time they had the animals safe, the water was halfway up the hawthorn hedge. It took three days for the river to go down, and another week before a man could walk the fields without sinking to his knees. The winter sowing would be late this year. There was no helping it.

The cooper was a patient teacher, and the boy he had taken on last Martinmas was beginning to show real promise. They worked together through the morning, shaping staves from the seasoned oak, bending them over the fire, drawing them down with the iron hoops. It was slow, careful work. By the bells for None they had a full barrel taking its shape on the floor. He had the lad run his hand around the inside to feel the evenness of the wood. The boy grinned. He did not smile back, but he nodded.

The fair at Stourbridge was the biggest of the year, and he had been saving all summer to make the journey. He left the village with the carter before dawn and reached the fairground by noon the next day. Two days of trading brought him a better price for his wool than he had ever had before, and he spent a little of the surplus on a pewter dish for his wife, which he hid in the bottom of his pack. He was never quite sure, afterwards, whether the journey had been worth the fuss. But the dish sat on their table for the rest of their lives.

He had been cutting peat on the common since the first thaw, and the stack beside the cottage was finally tall enough to see him through another winter. It was bone-tiring work, bent double in the wet turf, slicing the blocks with the long blade and carrying them one by one to dry. His back had been bad for years now. But the fire at home burned warm because of this labor, and his children slept easier because of it. A man did what he had to do. That was the whole of it.

The mare had foaled in the night, and he had sat up with her in the stable through most of it, half-asleep against the post with a lantern at his feet. By dawn there was a long-legged colt standing unsteadily in the straw, and the mare was licking him clean. He watched for a long time before he went back to the cottage to sleep. His son would want to see the foal later, and his wife would scold him for being up all night. He did not mind. He had not minded for years.

The war had reached their part of the country in the late summer, and the lord had called for men. He had gone without complaint, as his father had gone in his time. The fighting was brief and confused, and he came home in November with a scar above his eye, a bundle of plunder he would not speak about, and a silence that took some weeks to begin to lift. His wife did not press him. She put a clean shirt on him and set him to work on the winter wood, and in time he was himself again, mostly.

The hedge between his field and the neighbor's had been disputed for three generations, and the summer court was to hear the case again that week. He took his father's old testimony to the reeve, and the reeve heard him out with weary patience. The decision, when it came, went against him by a foot and a half. He cursed under his breath, paid the small fine, and walked home in the heat. Some things were not worth fighting forever. The neighbor, he noticed, did not gloat. That was something.

The baby had come in the middle of harvest, as babies often did, and he had been summoned from the field by a breathless neighbor's child. By the time he reached the cottage, the midwife had everything well in hand, and a thin, furious wail was coming from the bedchamber. He stood in the doorway with his hat in his hand, too dirty from the work to come closer. His wife smiled at him over the small bundle in her arms. That evening he went back to the field and finished the sheaf he had been working on when they called him.

He had always been the steadiest of the three sons, and when the old man finally died it was to him that the cottage and the strip of land came. His younger brothers took it without much argument. The elder was already set up with a forge of his own in the next village. The youngest had gone for a soldier years ago and was not expected to return. He took up the plough his father had guided and turned the soil for the winter sowing. The furrow was straight. His father, he thought, would not have been displeased.
She received the ambassadors in the solar, seated, her hands folded.
The coronet was lifted from a velvet cushion and placed upon her brow.
Her ladies followed her into the garden in a rustle of silk.
Eleanor signed the warrant and sealed it with her own ring.
She refused the match and let the silence stand.
The dowager entered the chapel first, as was her right.
Her embroidery was famous from Paris to Ghent.
She rode a white palfrey at the head of the procession.
The princess curtsied to her mother and took the scroll.
She dismissed her ladies with a single lifted hand.
Pearls had been stitched into the hem of the gown by candlelight.
She dictated the letter to her secretary without pausing.
Her name was read in the cathedral during the bidding prayer.
The empress descended the marble steps unhurried.
She placed the ring on the altar and withdrew without speaking.
The carriage bore the arms of her house in gilt.
She inspected the new chapel hangings by lamplight that evening.
Her favour was tied to the knight's lance before the joust.
She took the spindle from her daughter and corrected the thread.
The countess dined alone, as had become her habit.
She crossed herself before stepping into the litter.
The archbishop bent to kiss the hem of her mantle.
Her reply to the French queen was delivered in immaculate Latin.
She loosed the merlin from her wrist and watched it climb.
The book of hours lay open on the prayer desk where she had left it.

The regency had fallen to her when her husband went to the Holy Land, and she had not expected it to last more than a year. It had now been almost five. She governed from the great hall on Wednesdays and Saturdays, and the rest of her time was her own — or as much of it as a woman in her position could call her own. The barons had learned, slowly, that she was not to be underestimated. Her chancellor said so openly. Her enemies said so privately. Both of them were right.

The embroidered altar cloth had taken her women three months to finish, and she had stitched much of the central panel herself. The Virgin, the Magdalene, and a kneeling donor who bore her own features more than coincidentally. She presented it to the abbess at Candlemas, along with a purse for the convent's new building. The abbess was properly grateful. She accepted the gratitude with the grace she had been taught at seven, and she spoke warmly of the convent's recent work. Afterwards, in the carriage, she allowed herself the small pleasure of having given something beautiful.

She had been betrothed to the Duke of Savoy at the age of eleven, and she had met him for the first time at the age of fifteen, on her wedding day. He was thirty-eight, and kinder than she had feared. The first years had been strange. The children had come slowly, then all at once. Now, at thirty, she was the mother of four, the stepmother of two more, and the mistress of a court that was beginning, at last, to feel like her own. She was careful not to take any of it for granted.

Her husband's campaigns kept him abroad for most of the year, and the letters that came from the south were short and infrequent. She ran the estates in his absence, as was her duty and her right. The stewards reported to her weekly. The chaplain said the prayers for his safe return at Mass. The children asked less and less about him as the months went on. She did not hold this against them, or against him. It was the way things were, and she had long since stopped wishing they were otherwise.

The queen received the Scottish delegation in the smaller hall, as protocol dictated for a matter still formally under negotiation. She spoke French throughout, though she understood every word of the Scots' rough English, and the interpreter made the translations with her own coolness. The deal that emerged at the end of three days was not exactly what her council had wanted, but it was better than the alternative. She signed it in the late afternoon by the window. Then she went to the chapel and prayed for a long time.

She had been widowed in her twenty-sixth year, and she had declined all subsequent proposals of marriage with a politeness that masked her absolute firmness. The castle was hers, her son's inheritance was secure, and she had no wish to place either of those things under another man's authority. The suitors had continued to come, of course. She had continued to refuse. By the time she was forty, they had mostly stopped. She had come to think of that decade as the happiest of her life.

The princess had been ill through the winter, and the court had kept it as quiet as possible. She had spent most of the season in her private apartments, with only her dearest women and her physician. By Eastertide she was walking in the garden again, pale and thin but smiling. Her mother came to see her every evening without fail. Her father came twice. She did not hold that against him either. She was, after all, his daughter, not his heir, and he had a kingdom to attend to.

At the wedding feast of her youngest daughter she sat in the place of honour beside the king of France, and she held her own in the conversation through four courses. The new son-in-law was a decent young man, from a good house, with prospects. She had arranged the match herself over two years of patient correspondence. Tonight, watching the couple dance together in front of the assembled court, she allowed herself a moment of quiet satisfaction. Then she returned her attention to the French king, and to the more delicate matter of the northern border.

The book of hours her aunt had sent for her fifteenth saint's day was the most beautiful thing she owned. She kept it on the prayer desk in her chamber, and she read from it at Matins and at Vespers, as she had been taught. The illuminations were exquisite — roundels of the Annunciation, the Flight into Egypt, the Virgin Crowned — and the gold leaf still caught the candlelight after all these years. When she died, the book would go to her eldest daughter, and then to her eldest granddaughter, and then to whoever came after. It was that kind of thing.

The empress held her council on the fifth day of every month, and she held it personally, without her husband's presence. This had been the arrangement for over a decade, and the ministers had long since stopped finding it unusual. She listened more than she spoke, as was her habit. She asked sharp questions when she chose to. The decisions, when they came, were invariably hers, and the ministers knew it. Her husband ruled the empire in name. She ruled it in everything but. They had an understanding.

Her grandmother had taught her to ride before she was five, and she had ridden almost daily ever since. Even the strictest of her tutors had not been able to persuade her out of her morning gallops across the downs. At forty-two she still rode, though she now preferred a gentler mare than the mad chestnut of her youth. The wind on her face was, she believed, one of the very few things in her life that could not be taken from her by politics or by time. She intended to keep riding until she could not.

The coronation day was the longest of her life. She had been awake since before Matins, and the anointing, the crowning, the procession, the feast, and the long, slow receiving of homage had gone on for over sixteen hours. By the time she reached her bedchamber she was barely able to stand. Her women undressed her in near silence. She looked at herself in the polished silver glass for a moment before the gown came off, and she did not quite recognize the face looking back. She slept for twelve hours and woke up a queen.

She had kept a small correspondence with the abbess of St Mary's since girlhood, and the letters that passed between them were the most honest she ever wrote. The abbess was the one person in the world to whom she could admit weakness. She wrote of the pressures of court, the weight of her responsibilities, the fear she could not show in front of her ladies. The abbess wrote back with quiet wisdom and, occasionally, with sharp correction. The letters were burned after reading, per her instructions. But she carried their contents in her heart long after.

The plague year drove the court out of the capital in April, and she did not see her palace again until the following November. They moved from hunting lodge to hunting lodge, staying ahead of the sickness. Her youngest son fell ill at Michaelmas and recovered, by the grace of God. Others did not. When they finally returned, the city was smaller than it had been, and the gardens of the palace had gone to weeds. She ordered them replanted that spring, and she took personal interest in the work. There was something to be said for starting over.

Her daughter's marriage had been arranged when the girl was eight, and the contract had held for twelve years despite several attempts by rival factions to break it. The wedding finally took place in the autumn, and she gave her daughter away with a steady hand and a carefully composed face. In the carriage on the way back from the cathedral, she wept for a quarter of an hour without shame. Her lady-in-waiting, who had known her since girlhood, held her hand and said nothing. There was nothing to say.

The young king's mother came down to the stables to see him off herself, which surprised the grooms and pleased the king. She adjusted his cloak at the shoulder, told him briskly to write, and stepped back without any display of sentiment. He knew her well enough to understand what the small gesture had cost her. He bowed slightly, mounted, and rode out through the gate at the head of his column. She watched from the yard until the last of the horses had disappeared down the road. Then she went back inside and took up the morning's correspondence.

The countess had been widowed young, and she had managed the estates on behalf of her infant son for nearly twenty years. The land had prospered under her. The tenants paid on time. The castle had been rebuilt in stone in the second decade of her stewardship. When her son at last came of age and took up his inheritance, she handed him the keys at a small, private ceremony in the great hall, witnessed only by the chaplain and the steward. He tried to give them back. She refused, gently. The work had always been his to inherit.

Her husband, the king, had been absent at the wars for most of the summer, and the court had grown restless in his absence. She had taken to holding small evenings in her own apartments — music, poetry, occasionally a visiting scholar from one of the universities. The atmosphere was different from the king's court: quieter, more thoughtful, with a different sort of conversation. She was surprised to find that she preferred it. When the king returned in September with his victories and his loud companions, she took up her formal duties again without complaint. But she did not stop the evenings.

The young princess had been sent to the convent for her education at the age of six, and she returned to court at thirteen a different girl than the one who had left. She spoke three languages with ease. She read music. She could hold a conversation with a bishop without blushing. Her mother greeted her at the gates with a careful embrace and led her inside. The reintroduction to court life was managed slowly, as these things had to be. By Christmastide she had taken her place at her mother's side as if she had never been away at all.

Her mother had died when she was nine, and the memory had softened over the years into something like a warm shape at the edge of her mind. She had grown up in her stepmother's household, which was cold but not cruel. She had learned the art of being useful without being noticed. These were habits she had never entirely shed, even after she became a queen in her own right. Her ladies remarked on her quietness, her watchfulness. They did not know how much of both had been taught to her by a childhood she rarely spoke of.

The feast of Epiphany at her husband's court was the most splendid event of the year, and the planning of it began in November. Menus were drafted. Musicians were hired from as far away as Italy. Gifts were prepared for the children of the household, who would not forget them. She oversaw every detail herself, as was her preference, and on the night of the feast she stood beside her husband at the high table and greeted the guests with the composure she had worn like a second gown for twenty years. At midnight she finally sat down, and she accepted a cup of warmed wine from a page, and she allowed herself to rest.

She had never cared for the hunt, though protocol required her to attend often enough. What she loved, instead, was the walled garden behind the castle, which her grandmother had planted and which she had enlarged over the years. Roses, herbs, a small orchard of pear and medlar. She spent at least an hour there every day that the weather allowed, often with a book, sometimes with her children, occasionally alone. Her ladies understood that she was not to be interrupted in the garden unless a real matter called for her. The garden, she said once, was where she went to remember what she was for.

The dowager queen had outlived two husbands and three of her six children, and she bore it all with a composure that her grandchildren sometimes mistook for coldness. She was not cold. She was simply careful with her grief. Once a year, on the anniversary of her first husband's death, she went alone to the chapel and stayed there for the whole of the afternoon. Her ladies knew better than to disturb her. What she did in there, and what she thought, was nobody's business but her own. She emerged in the evening and resumed her duties without comment.

Her correspondence with the queen of Castile ran to over a hundred letters across twenty years, and the two women had never once met in person. They wrote about their children, their husbands, the state of their kingdoms, the progress of their gardens, the health of their aging mothers. Each of them knew that the letters were read by their respective chanceries before being sealed. Neither of them minded. Within the constraints of the medium, a real friendship had grown, and both of them treasured it, in their different ways, until the end.

The empress had been told of her husband's death at the hunting lodge, and she had insisted on riding through the night to reach the capital before the news could spread. She arrived at dawn, white-faced and mud-spattered, and she was in the council chamber before the bells rang Prime. The succession had to be secured. Her son was fourteen. The regency would be hers, if she acted quickly. She acted quickly. By the time the court woke to the news of the emperor's death, the mother of the new emperor was already on the throne of the regent.
She drew water from the well before the household stirred.
The loaves were in the oven by the time the bells rang Prime.
Margery wrung out the sheet and hung it on the hedge.
The spindle turned steadily between her thumb and forefinger.
She stirred the pot and tasted the broth with the back of the ladle.
The baby was asleep on her shoulder by the time the fire burned low.
She fed the hens at dawn, cursing the rooster as usual.
The pail was heavy by the time she reached the top of the path.
She knelt at the riverbank, beating the linen with a flat stone.
Alice mended the torn apron by the fire after supper.
She carried the eggs to market in a cloth-lined basket.
The herbs were drying in bunches from the kitchen rafters.
She pulled the wool between her fingers and set the wheel spinning.
The cow had been milked and the stools rinsed before sunup.
She kneaded the dough with the heels of her hands.
Her skirts were dark at the knees from scrubbing the step.
She traded a round of cheese for a length of blue thread.
The needle moved quickly through the coarse grey cloth.
She sang to the child as she stirred the curds.
The sickle was sharpened and ready for morning's work.
She swept the hearth clean before setting the morning fire.
The berries filled her apron before the sun was fully up.
She laid the reed on the floor for the winter months.
The wash was hung across the bushes by midafternoon.
She carried the infant on her hip and the pail in her free hand.

She had been up since before the cock, as she always was in harvest time. The bread was already baking by the time the household stirred. She fed the hens, drew the water, and had the breakfast on the table before her husband came in from the barn. The children ate quickly, she ate what was left, and by the time the bells rang Prime they were all of them in the fields. It was the longest week of the year. It was also, in its way, the best.

The washing took two full days every fortnight, and she did not know a woman of her station who enjoyed it. She and her elder daughter carried the baskets down to the river before dawn, and they worked the sheets and shifts against the flat stones with wood ash and hard hands. By midmorning her arms were numb. By noon the linen was spread on the gorse bushes to dry. They walked home in the warming sun, stopping to pick watercress for the pot. Her daughter chattered. She listened, mostly.

Her husband had been away at the carting for six days, and the farm was hers to keep. She milked the cow morning and evening, fed the pig, fetched the hens in at dusk, and slept with the kitchen door barred because she did not care for the road at this time of year. The baby was teething, and sleep came hard. By the time he came home on the Saturday, she was hollow-eyed and short-tempered. He had the sense not to mention it. He brought her a ribbon from the town. She tied it in her hair that night and did not complain.

The milkmaid had been at the dairy since she was twelve, and the work had become as natural to her as breathing. Before first light she was already in the cow-shed, the stool under her, her forehead pressed to the warm flank of the black cow. The rhythm of the milking calmed her, had always calmed her. The cats wound around her ankles waiting for their share. By the time the sky had pinked the horizon, she had three full pails standing on the flagstones and was carrying the first of them to the dairy.

The seamstress worked by the light of the window until the afternoon sun moved past the eaves, and then she moved her stool to the other side of the room and worked by the other window until dusk. Today's piece was a set of sleeves for the vintner's wife, and the linen was better than most of what came through her door. She took her time with it. She was paid by the piece, not the hour, but a good reputation was worth more than a few extra pennies on a fast job. By the time the light failed, the first sleeve was nearly done.

She had birthed six children, and four of them were still living. The eldest was married and gone to the next parish. The second worked with his father in the field. The two small ones were underfoot from dawn to dusk, a constant source of affection and irritation. On the feast days she went to Mass with all of them scrubbed and combed, and she sat in the same pew her own mother had sat in, and she was grateful, most of the time, for the life she had. Grief was a quieter thing now. It had not gone away.

The market day at the county town came every fortnight, and she walked the four miles in each direction with a basket of eggs, butter, and two rounds of cheese. By the time she reached the square the sun was well up and the stalls were busy. She set up in her usual spot, between the fishmonger and the woman who sold ribbons, and she did not leave it until everything she had brought was sold. She bought salt, lamp oil, and a small sack of barley to bring home. She also bought a sweet bun for the walk back, which she ate slowly and guiltily and did not regret.

The washerwoman had come in from the country at fourteen and had never left the town since. She worked in the back courtyard of the inn six days a week, with her hands in hot water from dawn until dusk. The innkeeper's wife was not unkind, and the work was steady. She had a room in the attic, shared with two other women, and a window that looked out over the roofs of the lane below. At night, when the noise from the tap-room had finally died down, she would sit by the window and listen to the rain on the tiles. It was a life. It was hers.

The baby had been restless all night, and she was bone-tired by the time the household began to stir. She nursed him in the rocking-chair by the hearth, the dog at her feet, and she dozed between his feedings. Her husband came in from the byre and did not disturb her. He made his own breakfast, fed the dog, and was out the door again before she had properly woken. When she did rise, she found a cup of small beer and a cut of bread waiting for her on the table. Such small kindnesses, she had learned, were the shape of a good marriage.

The autumn had been wet, and the harvest had come in late and poor. The tithe had still been taken in full. She stretched the flour with bran and then with boiled oats, and by the time January came the meals were thinner than she cared to acknowledge. Her husband had gone out twice that month with the other men to set snares, and they had not come back empty-handed. The baby still nursed. The older children had grown quieter at the table. Spring would come, as it always did. She told herself this, every morning, at the cold hearth.

She had been the midwife in her village for nearly twenty years, and she knew every mother and every child within four miles. When the call came, she went, day or night, in rain or snow. Most of the births she attended went well. Some did not. She had learned, slowly and painfully, to accept that she could not save every woman, every baby, every time. What she could do was be there, clean, steady, unafraid. She packed her bundle carefully each evening before bed, in case she was needed before morning. She was, often enough.

The feast of the village's patron saint fell in late June, and the preparations went on for weeks. She and the other women of the parish cooked enough to feed the whole village twice over. On the day itself she wore her best gown, the one she had sewn from the cloth her husband had given her five years ago, and she danced in the square after the procession. She was not young anymore. She still remembered how. Her eldest granddaughter watched from the side, wide-eyed, and she thought, perhaps, that she had done some things right.

Her mother-in-law had moved into the cottage after the old woman's husband died, and the adjustment had been difficult for everyone. The house was not large. Privacy was hard to find. She and her husband argued more that first year than in all the years before. But time had done its work, as it often did. The old woman had grown gentler. She had grown more patient. They cooked together now, some days, in companionable silence, and they watched the grandchildren together by the fire in the evenings. It was not the life she had imagined. It was a good one, nonetheless.

She had spun wool since she was six, and by the time she was thirty she could spin a thread as fine as any in the county. The merchant from the town came through three times a year, and he always bought what she had. The money she earned from her spinning was her own. She kept it in a small clay pot under a loose stone in the hearth, and she knew to the penny how much was in it. Someday, she told herself, she would use it for something particular. For now, the knowledge that it was there was enough.

The priest's housekeeper had been in service at the rectory for twenty-two years, and she ran the house more firmly than the priest himself. She cooked, cleaned, mended, gardened, and on Sundays she prepared the midday meal for whatever guests the father had brought home from Mass. She was sixty years old now, and her knees pained her in the damp weather. She had never married. She had no regrets to speak of. The rectory was, in every way that mattered to her, her home, and she would stay in it until she could no longer climb the stairs.

The lavender harvest came in the last weeks of summer, and the whole family spent the days in the field cutting the long purple stems and binding them into sheaves. She loved this work more than almost any other. The scent rose around her all afternoon, her hands smelled of it for weeks afterward, and the bundles drying from the rafters of the still-room perfumed the whole cottage through the autumn. They sold most of the crop to the apothecary in the town. The rest she kept for her own use, to tuck into the linen chest and to strew among the rushes when the winter got long.

Her daughter had been taken on by the lady at the manor as a chambermaid, and the girl had come home for her day off full of the strangeness of it all. The lady was kind but particular. The food at the servants' table was better than she had ever eaten. The bed was in a dormitory with five other girls, and there was always someone snoring. She listened, holding the girl's hand, and she remembered her own first place at fourteen, and she said what seemed useful and kept the rest to herself. The girl slept that night in her old bed, and left again at dawn.

The village wise woman lived alone in a cottage at the edge of the common, and she had been there for as long as anyone could remember. People came to her for salves, for tonics, for advice about their children's rashes and their husbands' aching backs. She was not paid in coin, usually. A dozen eggs, a bag of wool, a share of the pig at slaughtering time. It was enough. She knew the names of every herb that grew within five miles, and she knew which ones would help and which would not. The priest, who had once eyed her with suspicion, now sent patients to her quietly when his own prayers did not suffice.

The weaver's wife had taken over the loom after her husband's death, and the neighbors had talked, as neighbors will. She did not care. The work was good, the customers were happy, and the rent was paid on time. She had taught her two daughters alongside her, and the three of them worked together in the long front room of the cottage, the shuttles clicking back and forth from dawn to dusk. In a year or two the eldest girl would be ready to take a piece to the fair on her own. She was already proud of that. She did not say so, but she was.

She had never learned to read, and she had never particularly wanted to. Her mother had not read, nor her grandmother, nor anyone in her family that she knew of. The priest read the Gospel aloud on Sundays, which was enough for her purposes. Still, her youngest son had been picked out by the parish priest as a promising lad, and the priest was teaching him his letters two evenings a week. She watched him bent over the slate at the kitchen table, his lips moving silently, and she felt a strange mixture of pride and distance. He was going to be something other than what she was. It was what she had wanted for him. It still felt, in some small way, like a loss.

The long winter had been hard on her mother, and by Candlemas it was clear the old woman was not going to last out the year. She moved her mother into the warmest corner of the cottage, beside the hearth, and she sat with her in the evenings after the children were abed. They did not talk much. They did not need to. She spooned broth into her mother's mouth when she would take it, and she held her hand when she would not. The old woman died in April, on a morning with the first real warmth of spring in the air, and she was buried in the churchyard beside her own mother. It was a good death, as deaths went.

She and the other women of the village gathered every Thursday afternoon in the old hall beside the church to sew and to talk. The sewing was mostly mending, sometimes a piece of work for the parish. The talking was everything else. News of births, of illnesses, of whose son was courting whose daughter, of what the priest had said on Sunday and what he had meant. She loved these afternoons. They were the one time in the week when the work of her hands and the work of her tongue could run together without interruption, and she came home afterwards calmer and more herself than at any other time.

The inn had been her husband's before he died, and she had kept it running for eight years on her own. The tap-room was busy most evenings, the rooms upstairs were clean and plain, and the food was plain but generous. The regulars treated her with respect, which had not always been the case at first. She had earned it, slowly, with hard work and with a firm hand when she needed one. Her daughter helped her now, and her son minded the stable. The work was endless. The life was hers. On the rare evenings when she sat by the fire with a cup of her own small beer, she was grateful.

The fair at St James's tide drew people from all the villages around, and she had gone every year since she was a girl. This year she went with her husband and her three eldest children, and they spent the day wandering among the stalls, eating roasted chestnuts, listening to the traveling musicians, and haggling over the price of a new iron pot she had been wanting for a year. Her husband bought the pot for her in the end, a small luxury she had not expected. She kissed him on the cheek in the middle of the crowded lane, and the children pretended not to see.

She was the last of her generation in the cottage. Her husband had died the previous winter, her mother the winter before, and her sisters had all gone to other villages with their own husbands years ago. The cottage was quieter than she had ever known it. The garden still needed tending. The hens still needed feeding. The work was the same work she had always done, and she did it now alone. Her grandchildren came once a week from the next farm, and she cooked for them, and she told them stories, and she sent them home at dusk with their pockets full of apples. It was not the life she had pictured at twenty. It was, still, a life worth living.
